planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in the garden in fall, pressing lightly into the surface of moist, humus-rich soil beneath deciduous trees. Natural winter cold provides the stratification required for spring germination. Do not cover seed deeply — light contact with moist leaf litter is sufficient.

directions: Choose a spot beneath deciduous trees where the soil is rich, slightly alkaline, and consistently moist but well-drained. Sow fresh seed directly on the soil surface in fall, as seeds lose viability quickly and require a cold-moist period to break dormancy. Expect germination the following spring, with flowering typically not occurring until the third to fifth year from seed. Once established, sharp-lobed hepatica is long-lived and self-seeds slowly into a small colony; avoid disturbing the roots.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as new growth begins, ensuring each division retains several healthy roots and at least one growing point. Replant immediately at the same depth in humus-rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade. Water in well and keep consistently moist through the first season.

directions: Plant Japanese Hepatica in deep to dappled shade with fertile, humus-rich, reliably moist but well-drained soil; amend with leaf mold or compost before planting. Divide clumps carefully in early spring, replanting each section at the same depth and spacing divisions 6 to 10 inches apart. This variety resents disturbance, so handle the fleshy roots gently and water consistently until re-established. Named cultivars and double-flowered forms are best propagated only by division to preserve their characteristics.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring before flowering or immediately after blooms fade, splitting the crown into sections with both roots and foliage. Replant promptly in prepared, moisture-retentive soil in shade and water thoroughly. Cut back any tattered foliage after dividing to reduce transplant stress.

directions: Grow red lungwort in full to partial shade in soil enriched with compost or leaf mold, where it will spread steadily into an attractive groundcover. Divide clumps every three to four years in early spring to maintain vigor and prevent overcrowding, replanting at the same depth and watering well. The unspotted, velvety evergreen leaves are an ornamental feature year-round; cut back any damaged foliage in late winter before new growth emerges. Good air circulation and consistent moisture help prevent the powdery mildew that can affect lungworts in warm, dry conditions.

planting.note: Divide established clumps carefully in early fall after flowering, ensuring each section has healthy roots and a rosette of leaves. Replant immediately in a gritty, humus-rich, well-drained mix in a cool, lightly shaded position such as a trough or rock garden. Protect from winter wet with a pane of glass rather than mulch, which can cause crown rot.

directions: Alpine snowbell demands excellent drainage and cool, consistently moist conditions; plant in a trough, raised bed, or rock garden crevice filled with a mix of gritty sand, pea gravel, and leaf mold. Choose a position with morning sun and afternoon shade, or dappled light, to replicate its high-altitude habitat. Divide established clumps after flowering in fall to propagate, handling the shallow roots with care and replanting immediately. Winter wet is the primary threat to survival — shelter plants with a pane of glass over winter if prolonged wet spells are typical in your climate.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early fall once flowering is fully complete, keeping divisions small — one to three rosettes each — and replanting immediately at the original depth. Use a humus-rich, gritty, well-drained soil mix and firm in gently. Water in well and protect from prolonged winter wet.

directions: Mountain snowbell is slightly more adaptable to lowland conditions than alpine snowbell but still requires cool summers, excellent drainage, and consistent moisture through the growing season. Plant in dappled or partial shade in a rock garden, raised bed, or trough using a mix of loam, leaf mold, and coarse grit. Divide clumps every few years in fall after the foliage dies back, immediately replanting divisions in fresh, enriched mix. The leathery, overwintering leaves are sensitive to crown rot, so avoid mulching directly over the crown and ensure water drains freely away from the plant.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in autumn, pressing seeds just below the surface of moist, humus-rich soil in deep shade. Seeds undergo double dormancy and may take two or more winters to germinate; do not disturb the planting area between seasons. Mark the site clearly and maintain consistent moisture.

directions: Plant herb paris in deep shade beneath mature deciduous trees in moist, fertile, neutral to alkaline soil rich in organic matter, mimicking the undisturbed ancient woodland floor it naturally inhabits. Sow fresh seed in fall directly where plants are to grow, as double dormancy means germination may not occur until the second spring after sowing; patience is essential. Once established, plants are extremely slow-growing and strongly resent any root disturbance — choose the planting site carefully and leave plants undisturbed indefinitely. Note that the single dark berry produced by each plant is toxic and should not be consumed.
